title: Value Residual Learning For Alleviating  Attention Concentration In Transformers

reviews.summary: The paper proposes two transformer variants aimed at mitigating the training-time phenomenon the authors call attention concentration (progressive focusing of attention on a few tokens across layers) and improving KV-cache efficiency. The first, ResFormer, approximates cross-layer attention by adding a pre-attention residual on the values from the first layer V1 to each later layer’s values Vn, yielding Un = 1/2 An (Vn + V1). The second, SVFormer, further decouples values by reusing only the first-layer value across all layers, Un = An V1, reducing KV-cache by roughly 2×. The authors present analyses (entropy of token importance, value/hidden-state norms), ablations, and pretraining experiments on SlimPajama (20B tokens, up to 468M params), showing training/validation loss improvements over vanilla Transformer and some KV-efficient baselines, plus small gains on several zero-shot reasoning tasks.
strengths: The cross-layer attention approximation via duplicating K and averaging values is simple, elegant, and analytically justified (Eqn. 3→4), turning concatenation into a value-average under the same attention matrix.

ResFormer’s pre-attention value residual is a neat alternative to prior post-attention fusion (e.g., DenseFormer, NeuTRENO), with a clear mechanistic motivation (interrupt the value-drain/attention-sink feedback).
SVFormer proposes a clean, orthogonal KV-sharing strategy: share only values across layers (rather than keys-and-values together), which appears underexplored relative to MQA/GQA/CLA.

Comprehensive ablations: residual on values vs queries/keys; mapping choice (current-attention vs identity vs explicit cross-layer attention); which historical layer(s) to connect; sensitivity to λ; interplay with GQA; sequence length and learning-rate effects.
Useful diagnostic analyses (entropy of token importance, norms, eigenspectra) to support the claimed mitigation of attention concentration and value-state drains.
The cross-layer approximation and its derivation are presented clearly and concisely, making the core idea easy to understand and implement.
The conceptual unification of NeuTRENO and DenseFormer within the proposed framework helps situate the contribution mechanistically.

Attention concentration and KV-cache size are both salient, current bottlenecks. A low-overhead architectural modification that aids both interpretability/robustness (less attention sink) and memory efficiency (SVFormer) is potentially impactful.
The approach appears compatible with popular efficiency methods (e.g., GQA), making it a candidate building block for future deployments.
weaknesses: The cross-layer approximation (replacing Km with Kn) discards the possibility of distinct routing via earlier keys; empirical cosine similarities are moderate and tested on small scales, raising questions about generality at larger model sizes and longer training periods.

SVFormer fundamentally reduces per-layer representational flexibility by fixing V1; the paper acknowledges it needs ≈12.2% more parameters to match baseline validation loss and performs worse at shorter sequence lengths—practicality may thus be scenario-dependent.

Most results are at modest scales (≤468M params, 20B tokens, 10k steps) and rely heavily on relative training curves; more decisive comparisons (final perplexity on standard corpora; longer training; larger models) are needed to substantiate scaling claims.

Inference-time benchmarks of KV-cache reduction (wall-clock latency, throughput, bandwidth) are missing; claims about memory/computation parity for ResFormer and real KV/memory savings for SVFormer would benefit from end-to-end serving evaluations.
Downstream evaluations are limited and gains are modest; broader LM-eval or standardized perplexity suites would better substantiate the general utility.
Several typos/inconsistencies impede precision (e.g., the entropy interpretation contradicts earlier statements; stray “0.06” near Eqn. 10; some figure captions/axes are ambiguous).
The definition and measurement of “token importance” could be clarified and connected more directly to reported entropy trends and their statistical variability.

While GQA/CLA/MLKV are cited, the paper would benefit from a more detailed empirical comparison to CLA at similar KV budgets across more settings, and to recent cross-layer/value-sharing variants (e.g., MLA, multi-layer K/V approaches) using matched training protocols.

There appears to be a very close contemporary preprint with similar naming and core ideas (value residuals and SVFormer). The relationship to that work should be clarified and properly contextualized to avoid ambiguity over novelty.
reviews.questions:
1. How does ResFormer affect inference-time latency and memory bandwidth when repeatedly reading V1 at every layer? Could you provide end-to-end serving benchmarks (prefill/decoding throughput and latency) vs Transformer, CLA2, and GQA at comparable KV budgets?
2. The entropy definition states “the higher the entropy, the greater the clustering,” which seems inverted relative to the earlier statement that lower entropy indicates concentration. Please clarify the definition, normalization (causal mask handling), and how you aggregate statistics across heads/tokens.
3. How robust are the 10.4% parameter and 13.6% data reductions across random seeds, model widths/depths, and longer training? Can you include confidence intervals or multiple seeds?
4. SVFormer underperforms at short sequence lengths but improves at long ones. Can you quantify the training dynamics causing the “critical point” (e.g., gradients through Wv1, optimization difficulty of V1)? Would a learnable, layer-wise gate on V1 help reduce the performance gap at short sequence lengths?
5. Could you report perplexity on standard corpora (e.g., Wikitext-103, LAMBADA) and more comprehensive LM-eval suites to better ground the generalization claims?
6. What is the precise relationship between your submission and the contemporaneous ResFormer preprint with similar naming and core ideas? Please clarify the novelty and any new contributions (analysis, ablations, or results) introduced here.
reviews.assessment:
This paper presents a simple and well-motivated architectural modification—pre-attention value residuals—that yields consistent gains at modest training scales and offers a principled approximation to cross-layer attention. The diagnostics are compelling and help validate the intended mechanism (reducing attention concentration and value-state drains). SVFormer, while not universally better than alternatives, enriches the design space by decoupling values-only sharing and shows promise in long-context regimes and when combined with GQA.

However, the empirical evidence is currently limited to relatively small models and short trainings, with heavy reliance on relative training curves rather than definitive perplexity/benchmark improvements at scale. The KV-cache claims would benefit from rigorous end-to-end serving evaluations. Some clarity issues remain (entropy interpretation, minor equation/caption artifacts), and the relationship to closely related contemporary work needs to be made explicit. Overall, I view the technical idea as neat and potentially impactful, but I recommend strengthening the paper with larger-scale experiments, standardized evaluations, inference benchmarks, and a clearer novelty statement before it reaches the bar for ICLR.
